patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 4/4] cleanup: Remove NULL check from unconditional guards

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 0/4] cleanup: Remove NULL check from unconditional guards

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch removes unnecessary NULL checks from unconditional lock guards in the Linux kernel. The checks were dead code because the locks are guaranteed to be non-NULL at destructor time due to the way the guards are constructed and used. The patch updates several macros, including DEFINE_GUARD, DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_1, and DEFINE_CLASS, to remove these unnecessary checks. This change is expected to have a small performance improvement.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 2/4] genirq: Move NULL check into irqdesc_lock guard unlock expression

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 2/4] genirq: Move NULL check into irqdesc_lock guard unlock expression

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch moves a NULL check from the __DEFINE_UNLOCK_GUARD() macro to the irqdesc_lock unlock expression, making the handling of NULL locks explicit at the call site. This change is in preparation for removing the NULL check from the macro itself. The patch has no functional changes.

review_comments.author: Thomas Gleixner
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with an Acked-by. No specific comments or concerns raised.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Jun 02 2026 at 07:12, Dmitry Ilvokhin wrote:

> irqdesc_lock uses __DEFINE_UNLOCK_GUARD() directly with a custom
> constructor that can set .lock to NULL.
>
> In preparation for removing the NULL check from __DEFINE_UNLOCK_GUARD(),
> move the NULL check into the irqdesc_lock unlock expression, making the
> NULL handling explicit at the call site.
>
> No functional change.
>
> Signed-off-by: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>

Acked-by: Thomas Gleixner <tglx@kernel.org>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 3/4] cleanup: Annotate guard constructors with nonnull

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 3/4] cleanup: Annotate guard constructors with nonnull

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to annotate guard constructors in the Linux kernel with nonnull attributes, ensuring that the compiler warns when NULL is statically known to be passed. The approach involves re-declaring the constructor with __nonnull_args() and annotating it directly. This change follows the existing convention for attribute wrappers and avoids clashing with glibc's __nonnull() when kernel and userspace headers are combined.

review_comments.author: Miguel Ojeda
review_comments.summary: Confirmed that the __nonnull_args() macro is intended to annotate nonnull arguments, not the return value. No objections or concerns raised.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Jun 2, 2026 at 9:13 AM Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:
>
> Miguel, I dropped your Acked-by due to the rename. Went with
> __nonnull_args() (over __knonnull()). Happy to restore your tag if that
> spelling works for you.

I am fine with either, but thanks for the caution! :)

I assume `_args()` is meant as "the `nonnull` for the arguments, not
the return"?

Cheers,
Miguel

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 1/4] nvdimm: Convert nvdimm_bus guard to class

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 1/4] nvdimm: Convert nvdimm_bus guard to class

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch converts the nvdimm_bus guard from using DEFINE_GUARD() to a class-based implementation, which will eventually require non-null arguments for constructors. The change is made to accommodate this future requirement without introducing functional changes or breaking existing code that passes NULL to the nvdimm_bus lock/unlock functions.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v5 3/4] cleanup: Annotate guard constructors with nonnull
Re: [PATCH] mm: don't allow empty relative nodemask in mpol_relative_nodemask()
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Patch to prevent empty relative nodemask in mpol_relative_nodemask() function, fixing a bug that causes kernel panic when attaching tasks to an empty cpuset.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential bug in mpol_rebind_mm() where it's called with cs->mems_allowed, which can be empty. Suggested calling it with newmems instead.

review_comments.author: Farhad Alemi
review_comments.summary: Confirmed the bug and provided a reproducer that panics on an empty cpuset. Suggested calling mpol_rebind_mm() with newmems instead of cs->mems_allowed.
review_comments.raw_body:
Confirmed, with a standalone reproducer (attached); it panics linus/master
at e8c2f9fdadee. cs->mems_allowed can legitimately be empty
on v2 -- a freshly created cpuset child that never had cpuset.mems
written keeps mems_allowed empty (never initialized) while effective_mems
is inherited non-empty in cpuset_css_online(), and v2 allows attaching
tasks to it (the empty-mems guard in cpuset_can_attach_check() is gated
on !is_in_v2_mode()). So the non-empty guarantee holds for effective_mems,
not for the configured cs->mems_allowed; forbidding empty cpuset.mems
would break v2's inherit-from-parent semantics.

The reproducer enables +cpuset, mkdirs a child without writing
cpuset.mems, moves a task in, mbind()s a VMA with
MPOL_BIND | MPOL_F_RELATIVE_NODES, and offlines a CPU; the hotplug walk
then calls mpol_rebind_mm(mm, &cs->mems_allowed) with the empty mask and
folds modulo nodes_weight(*rel) == 0 (console logs attached).

The newmems instinct looks right: it's the effective, online mask the
task is actually allowed to use, guarantee_online_mems() keeps it
non-empty, and it matches cpuset_attach(), which already rebinds against
cs->effective_mems. The fix this implies:

  - mpol_rebind_mm(mm, &cs->mems_allowed);
  + mpol_rebind_mm(mm, &newmems);

I built the current base (e8c2f9fdadee) with and without this one-liner:
the unpatched kernel panics on the first cpu1 offline, while the patched
kernel runs the reproducer's 8 offline/online cycles cleanly, with no
divide error.

This regressed in ae1c802382f7 ("cpuset: apply cs->effective_{cpus,mems}",
v3.17), which moved cpuset_attach() to the effective mask but left this
rebind on cs->mems_allowed.

Happy to send this as a proper patch (Fixes: ae1c802382f7, Cc: stable,
reproducer) if you agree the cpuset side is right, or to test a
mempolicy-side fix if not.


Thanks,

Farhad Alemi
PhD Student
SEFCOM Lab @ ASU

On Tue, Jun 2, 2026 at 2:54 AM Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net> wrote:
>
> On Tue, Jun 02, 2026 at 11:19:49AM +0200, David Hildenbrand (Arm) wrote:
> >
> > According to the report [1] syzkaller can trigger it. There is no reproducer,
> > though.
> >
> > [1]
> > https://lore.kernel.org/all/CA+0ovCgxbZkXa+OU8w3s84R3KNPNxxRfmsNR-udh+afQBbGNmw@mail.gmail.com/
> >
>
> The actual implication of this report is that there is a bug in cpuset,
> not mempolicy.
>
>   mpol_rebind_mm+0x3ab/0x680 mm/mempolicy.c:569
>       ^^^ should never receive a 0-node nodemask ^^^
>   ...snip...
>   cpuset_update_tasks_nodemask+0x22e/0x340 kernel/cgroup/cpuset.c:2777
>       ^^^ calls guarantee_online_mems ^^^
>   ...snip...
>   hotplug_update_tasks kernel/cgroup/cpuset.c:3882 [inline]
>   cpuset_hotplug_update_tasks kernel/cgroup/cpuset.c:3985 [inline]
>
> Relevant code:
>
> void cpuset_update_tasks_nodemask(struct cpuset *cs)
> {
> ... snip ...
>         guarantee_online_mems(cs, &newmems); <<< critical call
> ... snip ...
>         while ((task = css_task_iter_next(&it))) {
> ... snip ...
>                 mpol_rebind_mm(mm, &cs->mems_allowed);
>
> Seems like maybe mpol_rebind_mm should be called with newmems, not
> cs->mems_allowed, though cs->mems_allowed should never be allowed to be
> empty, because that makes no sense.
>
> Just eyeballing it, I can't say whether calling with newmems is the
> right thing, or if mems_allowed should not be allowed to be empty, would
> have to dig in a little further.
>
> ~Gregory

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC][RFC PATCH v4 00/27] Private Memory Nodes (w/ Compressed RAM)
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Private Memory Nodes patch series, focusing on compressed RAM and memory isolation.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Identified issues with using __GFP_THISNODE for isolation and suggested using a nodemask for allocation. Proposed two possible scenarios for userland APIs: one where the driver is responsible for alloc_pages_node() calls, and another where mempolicy.c handles it via _mpol() alloc variants.

review_comments.author: Balbir Singh
review_comments.summary: Clarified the use of mbind() API for allocation and suggested using __GFP_THISNODE to get to the desired node. Asked why not use mbind() API's and questioned gating allocation/privileges via a /dev.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Jun 02, 2026 at 09:57:48AM +0100, Gregory Price wrote:
> On Tue, Jun 02, 2026 at 12:16:50PM +1000, Balbir Singh wrote:
> > On Sun, May 24, 2026 at 09:50:06PM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> > > 
> > > I'm debating on whether to include OPS_MEMPOLICY in the initial version
> > > if only because it's not intuitive how it interacts with pagecache. That
> > > needs more time to bake.
> > >
> > 
> > It makes sense to look at it and then decide if it makes sense.
> >
> 
> I am thinking i will ship without any OPS flags at all for now and the
> have the introduction of ops as a separate series.
> 
> > > alloc_pages_node() is the kernel interface
> > 
> > I was think we wouldn't need explicit flags and that allocations would
> > happen from user space using __GFP_THISNODE to the node or via a nodemask
> > based on nodes of interest. Is there a reason to add this flag, a system
> > might have more than one source of N_MEMORY_PRIVATE?
> > 
> 
> There's a few things to unpack here.  I discussed this many times on
> list and at LSF, but to reiterate.
> 
> 1) __GFP_THISNODE is insufficient to enforce isolation and otherwise
>    not particularly useful.  Additionally, from userland, it's not
>    something you can actually set.

I was thinking mbind()/mempolicy() is how we get to it. It already
accepts a nodemask.

> 
>    for node in possible_nodes:
>        alloc_pages_node(private_node, __GFP_THISNODE)
> 
>    In fact it's the opposite semantic of what we want.
>    THISNODE says: "Do not fallback back to OTHER nodes".
> 

That's why we need to control the fallback nodes carefully for
N_MEMORY_PRIVATE

>    The semantic we want is "Do not allow allocations from private
>    nodes UNLESS we specifically request" (__GFP_PRIVATE).
> 
>    __GFP_THISNODE does not actually buy you anything here, AND it's
>    worse, in the scenario where a private node makes its way into the
>    preferred slot (via possible_nodes or some other nodemask), the
>    allocator cannot fall back to a node it can access.
> 
>    __GFP_THISNODE cannot be overloaded to do anything useful here.

Let me clarify, I meant to say, let's use a nodemask for allocation
and __GFP_THISNODE gets us to the node we desire, if that is the only
node. My earlier comment might not have been clear.

> 
> 2) We're trying not to expose *ANY* userland APIs for this, at all.
> 
>    The ultimate goal here should be one of two things:
> 
>    1) fd = open(/dev/xxx, ...);
>       mem = mmap(fd, ...);
>       mem[0] = 0xDEADBEEF; /* Fault device page into page table */
> 
>       In this case, the driver is responsible for doing the
>       alloc_pages_node() call.
> 
>    or
> 
>    2) mem = mmap(NULL, ..., ANON);
>       mbind(mem, ..., private_node);
>       mem[0] = 0xDEADBEEF; /* Fault device page into page table */
> 
>       in this case mempolicy.c is responsible for doing the
>       alloc_pages_node() call via the _mpol() alloc variants.
> 
> Addition OPT flags (reclaim, compaction, whatever), would
> (optionally) allow mm/ to operate on the device memory with, for
> example, mmu_notifier callbacks to tell the device to invalidate
> whatever it's caching about that page.
> 
> This would all be relatively transparent the userland, all userland
> "knows" is that it's getting memory from a device (/dev/xxx) or a
> node it's otherwise aware of hosting device memory somehow.
> 

Why not use mbind() API's? Do we want to gate allocation/privileges
via a /dev?

Balbir

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] mm: don't allow empty relative nodemask in mpol_relative_nodemask()
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Patch aims to prevent empty relative nodemask in mpol_relative_nodemask() by rebinding mm policy against the effective, online mask (newmems) instead of cs->mems_allowed.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Identified the actual bug as being in cpuset, not mempolicy. Suggested that mpol_rebind_mm should be called with newmems instead of cs->mems_allowed.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Jun 02, 2026 at 11:19:49AM +0200, David Hildenbrand (Arm) wrote:
> 
> According to the report [1] syzkaller can trigger it. There is no reproducer,
> though.
> 
> [1]
> https://lore.kernel.org/all/CA+0ovCgxbZkXa+OU8w3s84R3KNPNxxRfmsNR-udh+afQBbGNmw@mail.gmail.com/
>

The actual implication of this report is that there is a bug in cpuset,
not mempolicy.  

  mpol_rebind_mm+0x3ab/0x680 mm/mempolicy.c:569
      ^^^ should never receive a 0-node nodemask ^^^
  ...snip...
  cpuset_update_tasks_nodemask+0x22e/0x340 kernel/cgroup/cpuset.c:2777
      ^^^ calls guarantee_online_mems ^^^
  ...snip...
  hotplug_update_tasks kernel/cgroup/cpuset.c:3882 [inline]
  cpuset_hotplug_update_tasks kernel/cgroup/cpuset.c:3985 [inline]

Relevant code:

void cpuset_update_tasks_nodemask(struct cpuset *cs)
{
... snip ...
        guarantee_online_mems(cs, &newmems); <<< critical call
... snip ...
        while ((task = css_task_iter_next(&it))) {
... snip ...
                mpol_rebind_mm(mm, &cs->mems_allowed);

Seems like maybe mpol_rebind_mm should be called with newmems, not
cs->mems_allowed, though cs->mems_allowed should never be allowed to be
empty, because that makes no sense.

Just eyeballing it, I can't say whether calling with newmems is the
right thing, or if mems_allowed should not be allowed to be empty, would
have to dig in a little further.

~Gregory

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/9] nfsd: fixes for locally-triggerable bugs

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 0/9] nfsd: fixes for locally-triggerable bugs

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/9] nfsd: defer vfree of compound ops to fix rpc_status UAF
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free bug in the NFSv4 server by deferring the freeing of compound operation buffers until after an RCU grace period. The rpc_status netlink dumpit walks in-flight requests and reads opnums from these buffers, but the buffers are freed synchronously elsewhere in the code. By using kvfree_rcu_mightsleep() to defer the free, the patch ensures that the dumpit can safely read the buffers without accessing freed memory.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/9] nfsd: hold rcu across localio cmpxchg retry
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server (nfsd) where a freed nfsd_file object can be reused, leading to dereferencing of freed memory. The fix involves holding RCU (Read-Copy Update) lock across a cmpxchg retry block in the nfsd_open_local_fh function, ensuring that the slab page backing the nfsd_file is not recycled until the RCU grace period elapses. This prevents a non-zero word at the nf_ref offset of the recycled object from making the refcount bump appear to succeed, and subsequently dereferencing new->nf_net and new->nf_file out of freed memory.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 3/9] nfs/localio: fix ref leak on nfs_uuid_add_file failure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a reference leak in the NFS/localio code by releasing caller-owned file and net references when nfs_uuid_add_file() fails due to racing with nfs_uuid_put(). The fix involves using an RCU pointer to safely drop these references, allowing the slot pair and underlying struct file, dentry, and inode to be properly cleaned up. This patch addresses a previously fixed issue that was reintroduced in a later commit.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 4/9] nfsd: guard nfsd_serv deref in nfsd_file_net_dispose
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the nfsd service thread loop where it attempts to wake up a NULL svc_serv pointer, leading to a dereference error. The fix involves loading the nn->nfsd_serv pointer into a local variable and only calling svc_wake_up() when it is non-NULL. This matches the pattern used in the sibling helper function nfsd_file_dispose_list_delayed(). The patch addresses an issue introduced by a previous commit that left file closing work to a work queue, which was fixed with this change.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 5/9] nfsd: widen nfsd_genl_rqstp address fields to sockaddr_storage
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes an issue in the nfsd kernel module where IPv6 addresses were being truncated when sent to userspace via netlink attributes. The problem occurred because the address fields in the struct nfsd_genl_rqstp were declared as plain 'struct sockaddr' (16 bytes), which is not large enough to hold an IPv6 address. To fix this, the patch widens these fields to 'struct sockaddr_storage', which can hold larger addresses. Additionally, it ensures that the full address is captured in memcpy calls and initializes the genl_rqstp stack variable to prevent leaking uninitialized data. This patch resolves a bug introduced by a previous commit and fixes an issue that could be exploited by unprivileged processes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 6/9] nfsd: fix refcount leak in nfsd_file_lru_add on insertion failure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a latent bug in the NFS server's file cache, where an incremented reference count is never released when inserting a file into the LRU fails. This causes a permanent inflation of the refcount, preventing eviction of the affected file cache entry for the lifetime of the nfsd instance. The fix involves adding a compensating refcount decrement on failure and changing the WARN_ON to WARN_ON_ONCE to prevent log flooding. Additionally, the patch returns early on failure to skip unnecessary calls to nfsd_file_schedule_laundrette().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 7/9] nfsd: fix fcache_disposal UAF by inlining dispose state into nfsd_net
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free (UAF) bug in the NFS daemon's file cache disposal mechanism by inlining the dispose state into the nfsd_net structure. The original code allocated a separate struct for the freeme queue, which was freed during per-net teardown, causing dangling pointers when accessed by other threads. By moving the spinlock and freeme list directly into the nfsd_net structure, the patch ensures that these fields have the same lifetime as the net namespace itself, preventing the UAF bug. The changes also simplify the code by removing alloc/free helpers and modifying the initialization and shutdown functions to work with the new inline fields.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 8/9] nfsd: hold net namespace reference for delayed-dispose nfsd_files
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free bug in the NFS server by ensuring that a reference to the network namespace is held until an nfsd_file is fully disposed of. The patch introduces a new flag NFSD_FILE_NET_HELD to track whether an nfsd_file holds a net reference, and modifies the nfsd_file_free function to release this reference before freeing the file. Additionally, it moves the call to put_net in nfsd_file_put_local to ensure that the net reference is released before the file reference, preventing potential stale pointer issues.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 9/9] nfsd: unify cleanups in nfsd_cross_mnt() exits
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the nfsd_cross_mnt() function to move all path_put() and exp_put() calls to a single location after the codepaths join, ensuring consistent ordering between these operations. This change simplifies the cleanup process in this critical function, reducing complexity and improving maintainability.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses several bugs in the NFS daemon that can be triggered by an unprivileged user or require administrative access. The patches simplify the filecache disposal mechanism, fix reference counting issues, and address a use-after-free bug. They also unify cleanup operations in nfsd_cross_mnt() exits and widen address fields to sockaddr_storage. The changes aim to improve the stability and security of the NFS daemon by fixing locally-triggerable bugs.

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH] sunrpc: fix uninitialized xprt_create_args structure

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 00/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 00/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 01/10] fuse: separate next request fetching from sending logic
series_items.patch_summary: This patch separates the logic of fetching the next request from sending it, making the control flow clearer and reducing unnecessary parameter passing. It removes duplicated code in fuse_uring_send_next_to_ring() and introduces a new function fuse_uring_get_next_fuse_req() to fetch the next request. The send operation is now handled by a separate function fuse_uring_send(). This change simplifies the logic for handling requests and improves maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 02/10] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying to ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers to a ring in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring implementation. It extracts the header copying into a new function called copy_header_to_ring(), which centralizes error handling and rate-limited logging, making the code more clear and maintainable. The patch modifies several functions that interact with the ring, replacing direct calls to copy_to_user() with calls to the new function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 03/10] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying from ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers from a ring in the fuse kernel module. It extracts this logic into a new function called copy_header_from_ring(), which makes it clearer and more centralized, and also handles errors and rate-limited logging. This change is part of a larger series of patches to improve the io-uring functionality in the fuse module.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 04/10] fuse: use enum types for header copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the interface for copying headers in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation by introducing enum types to identify which part of the header needs to be copied. This change will simplify both kernel-space and user-space header addresses copying when buffer rings are added, making it easier to manage and maintain the code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 05/10] fuse: refactor setting up copy state for payload copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the code for setting up copy state in the FUSE implementation of dev_uring by introducing a new helper function `setup_fuse_copy_state`. This function encapsulates the logic for importing user buffers and initializing the copy state. The existing code is simplified and made more modular, reducing duplication and improving maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 06/10] fuse: use named constants for io-uring iovec indices
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces magic indices 0 and 1 in the iovec array with named constants FUSE_URING_IOV_HEADERS and FUSE_URING_IOV_PAYLOAD, making the code self-documenting and preparing for future buffer ring support. The patch modifies several functions in the fuse module to use these new constants instead of hardcoded indices. This change improves code readability and maintainability by eliminating magic numbers and making it easier to understand the purpose of each index.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 07/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools (implemented as buffer ring)
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a buffer pool mechanism to the fuse io-uring implementation, allowing the server to pass in a contiguous allocation of buffers that can be used on demand by the kernel. This decouples the number of entries from the number of payload buffers required, reducing memory usage and enabling future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption. The server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec payload in the first sqe. The kernel manages buffer selection and recycling using a buffer ring data structure, which is partitioned into individual buffers according to the buf_size passed in at registration time.

review_comments.author: Baokun Li
review_comments.summary: Gave Reviewed-by

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 08/10] fuse: support registered buffers as buffer pools in io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for registered buffers as buffer pools in io-uring, allowing servers to use an io-uring registered buffer as the buffer pool and eliminating the overhead of pinning/unpinning user pages per i/o request. Registered buffers require enabling the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag and setting the FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFFERS init flag during registration. The patch also includes benchmark results showing a 10% throughput improvement for direct randreads, a 4% improvement for direct sequential reads, an 8% improvement for buffered randreads, and a 6% improvement for buffered sequential reads.

review_comments.author: Baokun Li
review_comments.summary: Gave Reviewed-by

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 09/10] fuse: add zero-copy over io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds zero-copy support to the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring implementation, allowing for direct access to client pages or page cache folios without intermediate memory copies. The kernel registers relevant pages into a sparse slot in the server's io_uring registered buffer table, enabling the server to operate on these pages directly using fixed buffer operations. This feature is gated behind CAP_SYS_ADMIN and FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFFERS, requiring bufpools and keeping configuration space small. Zero-copy allows the server direct access to client pages, eliminating the need for intermediary buffers or page cache folios.

review_comments.author: Baokun Li
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that io_buffer_unregister() is never called when a connection abort occurs during a zero-copy request, leaving the buf_table slot occupied and folios pinned until the io_uring ring is destroyed.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Joanne,

If a connection abort happens while a zero-copy request is in-flight,
io_buffer_unregister() is never called. The buf_table slot remains
occupied and folios stay pinned until the io_uring ring is destroyed.

So perhaps we need to add the corresponding cleanup in
fuse_uring_entry_teardown(). Otherwise looks good to me:

Reviewed-by: Baokun Li <libaokun@linux.alibaba.com>


Cheers,
Baokun

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a mistake in the io buffer unregistration code and agreed to fix it by adding the missing call to fuse_uring_req_end() in the abort path.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Baokun,

Thanks for reviewing this series.  That's a good point - the io buffer
is unregistered in fuse_uring_req_end() when a request is ended, but
the abort code calls fuse_uring_stop_fuse_req_end(), not
fuse_uring_req_end(). I'll add this in.

Thanks,
Joanne

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 10/10] docs: fuse: add io-uring buffer pool and zero-copy documentation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation for using io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy techniques. The documentation explains how to use buffer pools, which decouple the relationship between entries and payload buffers, allowing for more optimal memory usage. It also describes how fuse manages buffer pools internally through a buffer ring, enabling features like incremental buffer consumption and multiple buffer sizes per ring. This patch aims to improve performance by reducing unnecessary copies of data between user space and kernel space.

review_comments.author: Baokun Li
review_comments.summary: Gave Reviewed-by

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds buffer pools and zero-copy functionality to the fuse io-uring implementation, allowing servers to pass in a managed buffer pool that reduces memory usage requirements and enables future optimizations. It also allows for direct access to client pages or page cache folios without copying data through an intermediary buffer, requiring CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges. The changes are based on registered buffers and zero-copy, which can provide significant throughput improvements depending on the server's per-request latency and backing I/O characteristics.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about duplicated logic in fuse_uring_send_next_to_ring() and fuse_uring_send(). They simplified the logic by removing duplicate code from fuse_uring_send_next_to_ring(), decoupling request fetching from the send operation, and reducing unnecessary parameter passing. The patch removes 50 lines of code and adds 28 new lines.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the tight coupling between ents and buffers in fuse io-uring by explaining their new buffer pool design, which decouples them and allows for future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the overhead of pinning/unpinning user pages and translating virtual addresses per i/o request, explaining that registered buffers eliminate this overhead by pinning pages upfront. The author provided details on how to use registered buffers, including setting flags in the registration sqe and following standard protocol for using registered buffers.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the zero-copy implementation's complexity and overhead, explaining that it requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges and FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFFERS to prevent partially-optimized modes. The author provided detailed request flow diagrams for both write and read paths, highlighting how pages are registered and unregistered in the io_uring registered buffer table.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed Bernd's concern about registering ring entries without payloads, explaining that entries may be used for servicing requests even if there are less buffers than the number of entries, and that it would allow multiple entries to use non-overlapping regions of the same buffer concurrently. The author also mentioned that supporting multiple buffer sizes per ring and some requests not needing payload buffers justify decoupling entries from buffers.

On Fri, May 22, 2026 at 3:49 PM Bernd Schubert <bernd@bsbernd.com> wrote:

> >
> >> be pinned that way without loosing one page per entry)?  For zero-copy
> >
> > For the header pinning changes in my branch, the headers are one
> > contiguous region (not per-entry), which circumvents having to
> > allocate one page per entry.
> >
> >> we could register entries without a payload? Advantage here would be
> >> that that header buffer would determine max number of entries, only
> >> little memory would be needed for over-subscribed entries, but entries
> >> themselves could be registered dynamically on demand.
> >
> > For zero-copy, entries still may have payloads (though the buffers
> > will definitely not need to be as big as the max payload size!),
> > either for the non-zero-copyable args or if the request's args aren't
> > page-backed. Unfortunately I think many servers may not be able to use
> > zero-copy due to the CAP_SYS_ADMIN requirements, so I think it'd be
> > ideal to minimize the memory consumption needed for the non-zero-copy
> > path, through the buffer pool for the payload buffers.
>
> Well, my problem is still why would we need to register ring entries if
> they don't have buffers - why not just register less ring entries then.

Entries may be used for servicing requests even if there are less
buffers than the number of entries. Most requests don't use the full
payload buffer size, so for example with incremental buffer
consumption, this would allow multiple entries to use non-overlapping
regions of the same buffer concurrently. As well, if we want to
support multiple buffer sizes per ring, there shouldn't be a 1:1
mapping between ents and buffers. There's also some requests that
don't need to use payload buffers at all (eg flush, fsync, rmdir,
etc).  As well for registered buffers / buffer pinning, it is more
optimal for the buffer pool to be allocated contiguously and pinned
contiguously rather than passed in one by one as a 1:1 entry:buffer
mapping.

Thanks,
Joanne

>
> At least I do not see the use case for ring entries without buffers.
> And that is exactly where my question about keeping the possibility for
> dynamic ring entry registration comes from. If ring entries register the
> buffers themselves, every ring could be set up with a single ring entry
> first and only once ring entries start to get used, libfuse could
> register more based on demand.
>
> Thanks,
> Bernd

Which is what the buffer pool provides (decoupling buffers from
entries) if we choose to later add that in the future. With 1:1
entry:buffer mapping, each entry requires a buffer that supports the
max payload size at registration time.

>
> > consumption, this would allow multiple entries to use non-overlapping

> > support multiple buffer sizes per ring, there shouldn't be a 1:1
> > mapping between ents and buffers. There's also some requests that
>
> I can counter argue that fuse-server can just send ring entries with a
> different payload sizes - fuse-server could dynamically create as many
> ring entries of the right size as needed.

I don't think this is a good idea. The server would have to
guesstimate this as it doesn't know the request size until it sees the
request. The server could speculatively pre-register ring ents ahead
of time but there's no guarantee that would be the right size for the
ring entry either. And once added, if the workload pattern changes,
then the ent and allocated buffer may not be useful anymore. This also
adds (imo needless) complexity to the kernel side. Additionally, the
dynamic ring ents would in the ideal case have payload buffers pinned,
which there is not an non-messy way to do (for buffer pools, the pool
buffer pinning goes through io-uring registered buffers infrastructure
prior to being passed to fuse at registration time). Imo the better
way if you wanted to dynamically add ents would be to add a new buffer
pool to the ring.

>
> > don't need to use payload buffers at all (eg flush, fsync, rmdir,
>
> Could be handled by multi buf sizes as well.

These requests don't require payload buffers.

This series already handles this case (eg not assigning a buffer from
the pool to the request).

>
> > etc).  As well for registered buffers / buffer pinning, it is more
> > optimal for the buffer pool to be allocated contiguously and pinned
> > contiguously rather than passed in one by one as a 1:1 entry:buffer
> > mapping.
>
> I don't think how the buffer is passed to fuse-client/kernel matters for
> (userspace) allocation. I had written before that libfuse's buffer
> allocation is not ideal, I had it continuous in initial development,
> then run into bug and for simplicity changed to per entry allocation.
> Switching back to larger allocations is simple.
> Regarding page mapping/pinning, why does it matter if the buffer in
> kernel is known as per entry or per allocation (assuming payload buffers
> are page aligned)?

It does matter how the buffers are passed to fuse for page pinning.
Please take a look at patch 8 ([PATCH v3 08/10] fuse: support
registered buffers as buffer pools in io-uring) [1]. The buffer pool
is contiguously allocated and registered as an io-uring registered
buffer, and then passed to fuse and by that point, the buffers have
already been pinned by io-uring.

Even irrespective of page pinning, it would still be good to pass the
buffers contiguously up front as a pool instead of per-ent, as the
purpose of this series is to decouple the 1:1 entry:buffer mapping. Eg
if the request an ent is servicing does not require buffers, a buffer
should not be reserved for that ent, amongst other benefits of a
decoupled mapping.

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/fuse-devel/20260522205823.1597313-9-joannelkoong@gmail.com/

>
> I think the more important part for buf rings are pinned headers. For 4K
> pages the loss is maybe still acceptable and the remaining size could be
> used for small requests and I guess would have a perf benefit. For 64K
> pages the loss is quite a lot.

That is orthogonal to this series. This series focuses on payload
buffers, and header pinning is not part of this series as
bufpools/bufrings do not need pinned headers, as it makes no
difference for performance. As mentioned in the previous message, when
I was working on a separate series for removing the dispatch overhead,
I implemented header pinning as part of that, as that is when header
pinning becomes actually useful.

>
>
> Maybe let's summarize
>
> Bufrings
> - Fixed size, allocated at startup, cannot be easily increased or decreased
> - Allows 0 size payloads (FUSE requests that only need the header)
>
> Entries with payload
> - Allows more flexible setup - fuse server _could_ (not implemented as
> of now) create entries based on incoming IO.
>
> Neither provides real multi-size buffers per ring.
>

I think perhaps there is a misunderstanding of the patches in this series.

This series implements buffer pools, which decouple the 1:1 mapping
between ents and buffers that currently exists. Right now, ents and
buffers are tightly coupled where each entry has its own dedicated
payload buffer, requiring N buffers for N entries where each buffer
must be large enough to accomodate the maximum payload size.

This series allows servers to pass in a buffer pool where the buffers
are not statically mapped to an ent at registration time. This
decoupling reduces the memory usage requirements needed to use
fuse-io-uring. It is the necessary stepping stone for future
optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption (allowing
multiple entries to use non-overlapping regions of the same buffer
concurrently) and supporting multi-size buffers per ring.

Thanks,
Joanne

>
>
> Thanks,
> Bernd
>

It's not just about the memory savings. Fundamentally, I believe it's
the wrong abstraction to statically couple ents to buffers 1:1 as a
fixed mapping. Entries and buffers serve different purposes. Entries
handle request flow, buffers hold data. Statically coupling them 1:1
is inflexible, introduces unnecessary complexity, and imo has no added
benefit. This series decouples these two concepts. It allows
dynamically adding ents in the future as *generic* ents not tied to a
particular buffer size (eg the added ent can be used to service *any*
request size, not just the size of the buffer it's tied to). With
tying ents to buffers, the server is stuck with a static distribution
that can't adapt to workload changes.

> of different payload sizes provide the same feature as buffer pools and

Buffer pools / buffer rings make entries generic (can service any
size), whereas per-entry different sizes locks each entry to a
specific size. I don't think ents and buffers should be statically
coupled 1:1.

>
> >> Maybe let's summarize
> >>
> >> Bufrings
> >> - Fixed size, allocated at startup, cannot be easily increased or decreased
> >> - Allows 0 size payloads (FUSE requests that only need the header)
> >>
> >> Entries with payload
> >> - Allows more flexible setup - fuse server _could_ (not implemented as
> >> of now) create entries based on incoming IO.
> >>
> >> Neither provides real multi-size buffers per ring.
> >>
> >
> > I think perhaps there is a misunderstanding of the patches in this series.
> >
> > This series implements buffer pools, which decouple the 1:1 mapping
> > between ents and buffers that currently exists. Right now, ents and
> > buffers are tightly coupled where each entry has its own dedicated
> > payload buffer, requiring N buffers for N entries where each buffer
> > must be large enough to accomodate the maximum payload size.
> >
> > This series allows servers to pass in a buffer pool where the buffers
> > are not statically mapped to an ent at registration time. This
> > decoupling reduces the memory usage requirements needed to use
> > fuse-io-uring. It is the necessary stepping stone for future
> > optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption (allowing
> > multiple entries to use non-overlapping regions of the same buffer
> > concurrently) and supporting multi-size buffers per ring.
>
> I just don't see "reduces the memory usage" - there are few op code that
> do not need payload buffers. Do you see that exactly these op code
> disturb your workflow and you do need additional entries/buffers for
> those? I do see a lot of lookup/revalidate/getxattr, but not the op
> codes without payload. Equally I see IO that does not max payload for a
> lot of requests types - anything namespace related and reads/writes
> below max payload size.
>
> In the end, what I'm struggling with is that the "zero payload" is just
> one special case of different IO sizes and the entire code and flow is
> changed for this one special case, without a discussion forehand how to
> handle the other cases. And that one special case comes up rarely in my
> own testing and use cases.

I think we're talking past each other. Buffer pools aren't about "zero
payload" as a special case - they're about decoupling buffers from
entries in general. Zero-payload requests are one beneficiary, but the
design enables incremental consumption and multi-size buffers as
future work precisely because of this decoupling.

Thanks,
Joanne

>
>
> Thanks,
> Bernd

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing feedback on the buffer allocation mechanism in fuse io-uring, specifically the issue that most request types require less than the maximum payload size, leading to inefficient memory usage. The author proposes decoupling entries from payload buffers and using a pool of dynamically selected buffers instead. They are seeking input on this approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, May 22, 2026 at 1:59 PM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> This series adds buffer pools (implemented internally as a buffer ring) and
> zero-copy to fuse io-uring.
>
> Prior to this series, ents and buffers are tightly coupled where each entry
> has its own dedicated payload buffer, requiring N buffers for N entries where
> each buffer must be large enough to accomodate the maximum payload size. This
> is suboptimal as most request types require vastly less bytes than the maximum
> payload size and some requests do not require payload buffers at all.
>
> This series allows servers to pass in a buffer pool (internally managed as a
> buffer ring) that the kernel will use for servicing ents/requests. This
> decoupling reduces the memory usage requirements needed to use fuse-io-uring.
> It allows for future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption and
> supporting multiple buffer sizes per ring.
>
> This series also allows servers to use io-uring registered buffers as the
> buffer pool which eliminates the overhead of pinning/unpinning user pages and
> translating virtual addreses per i/o request.
>
> This series additionally adds zero copy to fuse io-uring. The server can
> directly access client pages or page cache folios without copying data through
> an intermediary buffer. This requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges and using
> registered buffers.
>
> This series has a dependency on io-uring registered bvec buffers changes
> in [1].
>
> The throughput improvements from registered buffers and zero-copy depends on
> how much of the server's per-request latency is spent on data copying vs
> backing I/O. When backing I/O dominates, the saved memcpy is a negligible
> fraction of overall latency. Please also note that for the server to
> read/write into the zero-copied pages, the read/write must go through io-uring
> as an IORING_OP_READ_FIXED / IORING_OP_WRITE_FIXED operation. If the
> server's backing I/O is instantaneous (eg served from cache), the
> overhead of the additional io_uring operation may negate the savings
> from eliminating the memcpy.
>
> In benchmarks using passthrough_hp on a high-performance NVMe-backed
> system, using registered buffers showed around a 10% throughput improvement
> for direct randreads (~2150 MiB/s to ~2400 MiB/s), a 4% improvement for direct
> sequential reads (~2510 MiB/s to ~2620 MiB/s), a 8% improvement for buffered
> randreads (~2100 MiB/s to ~2280 MiB/s), and a 6% improvement for buffered
> sequential reads (~2500 MiB/s to ~2670 MiB/s).
>
> Zero-copy showed around a 35% throughput improvement for direct
> randreads (~2150 MiB/s to ~2900 MiB/s), a 15% improvement for direct
> sequential reads (~2510 MiB/s to ~2900 MiB/s), a 15% improvement for
> buffered randreads (~2100 MiB/s to ~2470 MiB/s), and a 10% improvement
> for buffered sequential reads (~2500 MiB/s to ~2750 MiB/s). I didn't see
> enough of a clear improvement for writes due to write latency being I/O
> dominated.
>
> The benchmarks were run using:
>   fio --name=test_run --ioengine=sync --rw=rand{read,write} --bs=1M
>   --size=1G --numjobs=2 --ramp_time=30 --group_reporting=1
>
> The libfuse changes can be found in [2]. To test the server, run:
> sudo ~/libfuse/build/example/passthrough_hp ~/src ~/mounts/tmp
> --nopassthrough -o io_uring_zero_copy -o io_uring_q_depth=8
> Once this series is merged, the libfuse changes will be tidied up and
> submitted upstream.
>
> Thanks,
> Joanne
>
> [1] https://lore.kernel.org/io-uring/20260402160929.2749744-1-joannelkoong@gmail.com/T/#t
> [2] https://github.com/joannekoong/libfuse/commits/zero_copy_v3/
>

Miklos,

This series has stalled on an architectural question and I'd
appreciate your input on the direction.

Background:
In fuse io-uring, entries are reusable communication slots between the
kernel and the daemon. When a request needs to be sent from the kernel
to the daemon, it is assigned to an available entry. The entry carries
the request to userspace, and the response comes back through the same
entry. The number of entries determines how many requests can be
serviced concurrently. Currently, when the daemon registers an entry
during fuse io-uring setup, it passes in a payload buffer (sized to
the maximum payload, typically 1 MiB) alongside it. The buffer is tied
to the entry for the lifetime of the connection.

Problem that needs to be solved:
Most request types (lookup, getattr, open, etc) require far less than
the maximum payload size, and some (flush, fsync, etc) require no
payload buffer at all. Every entry reserves a full 1 MiB buffer
regardless of actual need. On systems with many entries, this memory
cost scales linearly - for example, 64 entries across 8 queues
requires 512 MiB of payload buffers alone.

Approach A:
Decouple entries from payload buffers. Entries become generic - any
entry can use any buffer from the pool. At setup time, the daemon
passes in a contiguous pool of memory, and the kernel dynamically
selects and recycles buffers from this pool as entries are assigned
requests. Entries servicing requests with no payloads consume no
buffer, and the pool can have fewer buffers than entries. This
supports adding multi-size buffers and incremental buffer consumption
(multiple entries using non-overlapping regions of the same buffer
concurrently) as optimizations in the future.

Approach B:
Keep the 1:1 entry-buffer coupling, but allow entries to be registered
with different payload buffer sizes at setup time. The daemon would
register entries with smaller buffers for metadata-heavy workloads and
larger buffers for data-heavy ones. This keeps the existing model of
passing in a payload buffer during entry registration at setup time
and requires no uapi additions. Each entry registration adds buffer
capacity whereas with buffer pools, buffer capacity would have to be
added as a separate pool.

I want to make sure I'm representing both approaches fairly - Bernd,
please correct anything I've gotten wrong.

Miklos, what are your thoughts?

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the need for CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges and FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFFERS, explaining that zero-copy allows direct access to client pages or page cache folios without copying data through an intermediary buffer, and that gating on registered buffers keeps configuration space small. The author confirmed that this is gated behind CAP_SYS_ADMIN because it allows the server direct access to the client's underlying pages.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Jun 2, 2026 at 2:24 AM Baokun Li <libaokun@linux.alibaba.com> wrote:
>
> On 2026/5/23 04:58, Joanne Koong wrote:
> > Implement zero-copy for fuse io-uring, eliminating memory copies between
> > userspace, the kernel, and the fuse server for read/write operations.
> > The server can directly access client pages or page cache folios without
> > copying data through an intermediary buffer. When a fuse request arrives,
> > the kernel registers the relevant pages into a sparse slot in the
> > server's io_uring registered buffer table. The server can then operate
> > on these pages directly using io-uring fixed buffer operations (eg
> > read_fixed/write_fixed) and the kernel unregisters these pages when the
> > request completes. Non-page-backed args (eg op out headers) will go
> > through the payload buffer as normal.
> >
> > This requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN and FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFFERS (which
> > requires using bufpools). Gating on registered buffers keeps the
> > configuration space small and avoids partially-optimized modes that are
> > unlikely to be useful in practice. This is gated behind CAP_SYS_ADMIN
> > because zero-copy allows the server direct access to the client's
> > underlying pages, rather than operating on an intermediary buffer that
> > the contents of the client's pages were copied into or on page cache
> > folios.
> >
> > The request flow for the zero-copy write path (client writes data,
> > server reads it) is as follows:
> > =======================================================================
> > |  Kernel                                   |  FUSE server
> > |                                           |
> > |  "write(fd, buf, 1MB)"                    |
> > |                                           |
> > |  >sys_write()                             |
> > |    >fuse_file_write_iter()                |
> > |      >fuse_send_one()                     |
> > |        [req->args->in_pages = true]       |
> > |        [folios hold client write data]    |
> > |                                           |
> > |  >fuse_uring_copy_to_ring()               |
> > |    >copy_header_to_ring(IN_OUT)           |
> > |      [memcpy fuse_in_header]              |
> > |    >copy_header_to_ring(OP)               |
> > |      [memcpy write_in header]             |
> > |                                           |
> > |    >fuse_uring_args_to_ring()             |
> > |      >setup_fuse_copy_state()             |
> > |        [skip_folio_copy = true]           |
> > |                                           |
> > |      >fuse_uring_set_up_zero_copy()       |
> > |        [folio_get for each client folio]  |
> > |        [build bio_vec array from folios]  |
> > |        >io_buffer_register_bvec()         |
> > |          [register pages at
> >                  ent->zero_copy_index]      |
> > |        [ent->zero_copied = true]          |
> > |                                           |
> > |      >fuse_copy_args()                    |
> > |        [skip_folio_copy => return 0       |
> > |         for page arg, skip data copy]     |
> > |                                           |
> > |    >copy_header_to_ring(RING_ENT)         |
> > |      [memcpy ent_in_out]                  |
> > |    >io_uring_cmd_done()                   |
> > |                                           |
> > |                                           | [CQE received]
> > |                                           |
> > |                                           | [issue io_uring READ at
> > |                                           |  ent->zero_copy_index]
> > |                                           | [reads directly from
> > |                                           |client's pages (ZERO_COPY)]
> > |                                           |
> > |                                           | [write data to backing
> > |                                           | store]
> > |                                           |  [submit COMMIT AND FETCH]
> > |                                           |
> > |  >fuse_uring_commit_fetch()               |
> > |    >fuse_uring_commit()                   |
> > |      >fuse_uring_copy_from_ring()         |
> > |    >fuse_uring_req_end()                  |
> > |      >io_buffer_unregister(ent->zero_copy_index) |
> > |        [unregister sparse buffer]         |
> > |      >fuse_zero_copy_release()            |
> > |        [folio_put for each folio]         |
> > |      [ent->zero_copied = false]           |
> > |      >fuse_request_end()                  |
> > |        [wake up client]                   |
> >
> > The zero-copy read path is analogous.
> >
> > Some requests may have both page-backed args and non-page-backed args.
> > For these requests, the page-backed args are zero-copied while the
> > non-page-backed args are copied to the buffer selected from the buffer
> > ring:
> >     zero-copy: pages registered via io_buffer_register_bvec()
> >     non-page-backed: copied to payload buffer via fuse_copy_args()
> >
> > For a request whose payload is zero-copied, the
> > registration/unregistration path looks like:
> >
> >     register:  fuse_uring_set_up_zero_copy()
> >                  folio_get() for each folio
> >                  io_buffer_register_bvec(ent->zero_copy_index)
> >
> >     unregister: fuse_uring_req_end()
> >                   io_buffer_unregister(ent->zero_copy_index)
> >                   -> fuse_zero_copy_release() callback
> >                      folio_put() for each folio
> >
> > The throughput improvement from zero-copy depends on how much of the
> > per-request latency is spent on data copying vs backing I/O. When
> > backing I/O dominates, the saved memcpy is a negligible fraction of
> > overall latency. Please also note that for the server to read/write
> > into the zero-copied pages, the read/write must go through io-uring
> > as an IORING_OP_READ_FIXED / IORING_OP_WRITE_FIXED operation. If the
> > server's backing I/O is instantaneous (eg served from cache), the
> > overhead of the additional io_uring operation may negate the savings
> > from eliminating the memcpy.
> >
> > In benchmarks using passthrough_hp on a high-performance NVMe-backed
> > system, zero-copy showed around a 35% throughput improvement for direct
> > randreads (~2150 MiB/s to ~2900 MiB/s), a 15% improvement for direct
> > sequential reads (~2510 MiB/s to ~2900 MiB/s), a 15% improvement for
> > buffered randreads (~2100 MiB/s to ~2470 MiB/s), and a 10% improvement
> > for buffered sequential reads (~2500 MiB/s to ~2750 MiB/s).
> >
> > The benchmarks were run using:
> >   fio --name=test_run --ioengine=sync --rw=rand{read,write} --bs=1M
> >   --size=1G --numjobs=2 --ramp_time=30 --group_reporting=1
> >
> > Reviewed-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> > Signed-off-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>
>
> Hi Joanne,
>
> If a connection abort happens while a zero-copy request is in-flight,
> io_buffer_unregister() is never called. The buf_table slot remains
> occupied and folios stay pinned until the io_uring ring is destroyed.
>
> So perhaps we need to add the corresponding cleanup in
> fuse_uring_entry_teardown(). Otherwise looks good to me:
>
> Reviewed-by: Baokun Li <libaokun@linux.alibaba.com>

Hi Baokun,

Thanks for reviewing this series.  That's a good point - the io buffer
is unregistered in fuse_uring_req_end() when a request is ended, but
the abort code calls fuse_uring_stop_fuse_req_end(), not
fuse_uring_req_end(). I'll add this in.

Thanks,
Joanne

>
>
> Cheers,
> Baokun

---

On Tue, Jun 2, 2026 at 2:37 AM Baokun Li <libaokun@linux.alibaba.com> wrote:
>
> On 2026/5/23 04:58, Joanne Koong wrote:
> > Add documentation for fuse over io-uring usage of buffer pools and
> > zero-copy.
> >
> > Reviewed-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> > Signed-off-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>
>
> Looks good to me, just one nit below:
>
> Reviewed-by: Baokun Li <libaokun@linux.alibaba.com>
>
> > ---
> >  .../filesystems/fuse/fuse-io-uring.rst        | 194 ++++++++++++++++++
> >  1 file changed, 194 insertions(+)
> >
> > diff --git a/Documentation/filesystems/fuse/fuse-io-uring.rst b/Documentation/filesystems/fuse/fuse-io-uring.rst
> > index d73dd0dbd238..79003ddd44fe 100644
> > --- a/Documentation/filesystems/fuse/fuse-io-uring.rst
> > +++ b/Documentation/filesystems/fuse/fuse-io-uring.rst
> > @@ -95,5 +95,199 @@ Sending requests with CQEs
> >   |    <fuse_unlink()                         |
> >   |  <sys_unlink()                            |
> >
> > +Buffer pools
> > +============
> >
> > +Buffer pools decouple the 1:1 relationship between ents and payload buffers,
> > +which allow for more optimal usage of memory. Entries only 'reserve' a buffer
> > +when actively processing a request that requires a payload buffer. This
> > +decoupling also allows for optimizations such as incremental buffer
> > +consumption which will allow non-overlapping regions of a single buffer to be
> > +used simultaneously across multiple ents/requests, and the possibility of
> > +multiple buffer sizes to be supported per ring in the future.
> >
> > +To use this, the server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during
> > +registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec payload in
> > +in the first sqe. The server needs to pass init.buf_size and init.nr_bufs
>
> Nit: "the iovec payload in in the first sqe" has a duplicated "in".

I will fix this up for v4. Thanks for taking a look at this.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: Bernd Schubert questioned whether header pinning is still possible, noting that if it's not done, io_uring_cmd_complete_in_task() cannot be avoided, resulting in a context switch for sync requests. He also proposed an alternative approach where the payload would be registered per ring entry and only the header would become a buffer ring.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Joanne,

On 5/22/26 23:14, Joanne Koong wrote:
> On Fri, May 22, 2026 at 1:59\u202fPM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>>
>> Changelog
>> ---------
>> v2: https://lore.kernel.org/linux-fsdevel/20260402162840.2989717-1-joannelkoong@gmail.com/
>> v2 -> v3:
>> * Rework the uapis to be more ergonomic. Use io-uring registered buffers
>>   infrastructure instead of doing pinning logic in fuse. Get rid of header
>>   pinning as that makes no real perf difference
>> * Rename from "buffer ring" to buffer pool. Logic is the same, just different
>>   naming

short question, I didn't check the code is header pinning still
*possible*? The problem if we do not pin headers is that
io_uring_cmd_complete_in_task() cannot be avoided, as the application
cannot write into fuse-server buffers and headers without pre-pinned
pages. And io_uring_cmd_complete_in_task() basically means a context
switch for all sync requests.

> 
> Just to reiterate, the logic is the same between this version and the
> previous versions. I just renamed it to "buffer pool" to decouple the
> implementation detail from the concept since it's uapi facing. The
> buffer pool is managed as a buffer ring internally.
> 
> Bernd, it would be much appreciated if you could take a look at this
> series when you have the time. I'm targeting the June merge window and
> the io-uring dependent changes have already been queued up in Jens's
> tree since the last merge window.

I will try and right is probably a good time, almost through with
libfuse sync-init (remaining are bugs), Darricks series is also almost
ready. Although I don't promise this weekend - reserved for Darrick and
other libfuse changes.

One piece I had been pondering a bit about, though did not dive into the
code yet.
What is if payload would be registered per ring entry (as without your
changes) and just the header would become a buffer ring (so that could
be pinned that way without loosing one page per entry)?  For zero-copy
we could register entries without a payload? Advantage here would be
that that header buffer would determine max number of entries, only
little memory would be needed for over-subscribed entries, but entries
themselves could be registered dynamically on demand.


Thanks,
Bernd

Thanks,
Bernd

Well, my problem is still why would we need to register ring entries if
they don't have buffers - why not just register less ring entries then.

At least I do not see the use case for ring entries without buffers.
And that is exactly where my question about keeping the possibility for
dynamic ring entry registration comes from. If ring entries register the
buffers themselves, every ring could be set up with a single ring entry
first and only once ring entries start to get used, libfuse could
register more based on demand.

Thanks,
Bernd

> 
> Entries may be used for servicing requests even if there are less
> buffers than the number of entries. Most requests don't use the full
> payload buffer size, so for example with incremental buffer

I think that is an argument for multi-size bufs per ring.

> consumption, this would allow multiple entries to use non-overlapping
> regions of the same buffer concurrently. As well, if we want to
> support multiple buffer sizes per ring, there shouldn't be a 1:1
> mapping between ents and buffers. There's also some requests that

I can counter argue that fuse-server can just send ring entries with a
different payload sizes - fuse-server could dynamically create as many
ring entries of the right size as needed.

> don't need to use payload buffers at all (eg flush, fsync, rmdir,

Could be handled by multi buf sizes as well.

> etc).  As well for registered buffers / buffer pinning, it is more
> optimal for the buffer pool to be allocated contiguously and pinned
> contiguously rather than passed in one by one as a 1:1 entry:buffer
> mapping.

I don't think how the buffer is passed to fuse-client/kernel matters for
(userspace) allocation. I had written before that libfuse's buffer
allocation is not ideal, I had it continuous in initial development,
then run into bug and for simplicity changed to per entry allocation.
Switching back to larger allocations is simple.
Regarding page mapping/pinning, why does it matter if the buffer in
kernel is known as per entry or per allocation (assuming payload buffers
are page aligned)?

I think the more important part for buf rings are pinned headers. For 4K
pages the loss is maybe still acceptable and the remaining size could be
used for small requests and I guess would have a perf benefit. For 64K
pages the loss is quite a lot.


Maybe let's summarize

Bufrings
- Fixed size, allocated at startup, cannot be easily increased or decreased
- Allows 0 size payloads (FUSE requests that only need the header)

Entries with payload
- Allows more flexible setup - fuse server _could_ (not implemented as
of now) create entries based on incoming IO.

Neither provides real multi-size buffers per ring.



Thanks,
Bernd
